ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 11713004 refers to water as a substance; used as closest parent since no specific bottled still water product code exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 500ml bottle of still water, equivalent to approximately 500g, as commonly sold in restaurants and convenience stores in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Water, bottled, generic — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 14555)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for bottled water (generic) used. Values reflect pure water composition: zero macronutrients, zero energy, negligible minerals. This is consistent with all approved sources for plain still water.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Water — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed convention
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure still water contains no glycemic carbohydrates and therefore has no measurable glycemic index. By established nutritional convention, water is assigned a GI of effectively zero. Per the zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value) and gi_category is set to 'low'. Glycemic load per 500ml serving is 0.0 since carbohydrate_g is 0.

hypertension.guidance: Bottled still water contains approximately 2mg sodium per 100ml and is freely recommended for individuals with hypertension as it contributes negligibly to daily sodium intake and supports adequate hydration.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Bottled still water contains no carbohydrates, no sugars, and has a glycemic load of zero per serving, making it the preferred beverage choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero cholesterol, zero sodium contribution
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bottled still water contains no fat, no cholesterol, and negligible sodium, and is universally recommended as the primary hydration source for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bottled still water (500ml) is commercially packaged plain water with no carbonation, no added flavours, no sugars, and no calories. It is sourced from natural springs, municipal water supplies, or purified water sources and undergoes minimal processing such as filtration and UV treatment before bottling. The water is clear, odourless, and tasteless by nature. Nutritionally, it contains zero energy, zero macronutrients (protein, fat, carbohydrate), and zero sugars, making it the ideal beverage for all dietary patterns including low-calorie, diabetic, cardiovascular, and weight-management diets. Trace minerals such as calcium, magnesium, potassium, and sodium may be present in very small amounts depending on the source, but these are nutritionally negligible. In Singapore, bottled still water is widely available in restaurants, hawker centres, cafes, and convenience stores, and is commonly served alongside meals. It is the recommended beverage for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease due to its complete absence of glycemic carbohydrates, sodium, and fat. The 500ml bottle is the standard single-serve format sold across food service and retail channels.
common_user_queries: How many calories in bottled water?
Is bottled water good for diabetics?
Is still water the same as mineral water?
Can I drink bottled water if I have high blood pressure?
How much sodium is in bottled water?
What is the glycemic index of water?

confidence_reason: Nutritional values for pure still water are universally established and confirmed by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 14555). Water contains zero macronutrients by nature, and this is consistent across all approved sources. No estimation or analogy was required for nutrient values.
notes: energy_kcal is naturally zero as pure water contains no caloric macronutrients. protein_g is naturally zero as water contains no protein. fat_g is naturally zero as water contains no fat. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as water contains no carbohydrates. sugar_g is naturally zero. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero. All vitamin and most mineral values are 0.0 as confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for bottled water; trace amounts of sodium (2mg), potassium (1mg), calcium (2mg), and magnesium (1mg) per 100g are reported as typical for bottled still water. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are confirmed zero by product formulation. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention. The cuisine tag 'Italian' in the input record reflects the restaurant context (Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore) and is not a property of the food itself; cuisine has been corrected to 'Generic' and region_of_origin to 'Global' as bottled still water is a universal product. No multilingual local names are required as this is a generic global product not associated with any specific cuisine requiring mandatory language coverage under the Veritas multilingual rules. The 500ml serving size and SGD 4.91 price point are preserved from the field survey data source record for context.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected cuisine from 'Italian' (restaurant context artifact) to 'Generic' and region_of_origin to 'Global'.
Set all core macros to 0.0 with explicit zero justification phrases in notes field per zero-macro justification rule.
Set glycemic_index to 1 (not null, not 0) per zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule; gi_category set to 'low'.
Populated ontology with defensible FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT entries using only permitted match_type values.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Wrote natural_language_description exceeding 200 characters with full clinical and nutritional context.
Ensured all aliases_by_language strings appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Documented all zero values and sentinel conventions in notes field.

Set sodium_mg to 2.0, potassium_mg to 1.0, calcium_mg to 2.0, magnesium_mg to 1.0 per USDA SR Legacy trace mineral data for bottled water rather than using -1 sentinel, since USDA does report these values.
